summary: Demand evidence agents completed and PRD readiness passed.

demand_status.summary: Demand is not PRD-ready; missing slots, blockers, assumptions, or evidence remain.

triage.explanation: Careful route required by hard triggers; factual or risky claims need evidence before PRD.

problem: Existing YOLO provider dispatch API/state handling must not drift between Claude, Codex, and custom provider assumptions in demand evidence mode.
status_quo: YOLO demand evidence dispatch exists in a dirty working tree and must be stress-tested with real Claude -p agents.
desired_outcome: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.
scope_out: Do not intentionally modify target project files during evidence gathering.
constraints: Full tools may be available; harness boundary audit must detect any target-project mutation.
acceptance_criteria: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.
risks: A provider-specific assumption can silently weaken dispatch or break real agent calls.

protocol.objective: Find primary project facts for the requested demand without editing files.
protocol.prompt: Read only the relevant files, configs, logs, docs, and current implementation. Return claims with exact paths/lines or source summaries. Separate assumptions from facts.

evidence_tasks.reason: Factual project claims need primary evidence.

protocol.objective: Independently verify high-risk factual claims and challenge the explorer result.
protocol.prompt: Use independent reads or sources. Confirm, weaken, or contradict each claim. Any conflict must become a blocker instead of being averaged away.
protocol.required_when: evidence_policy === cross_check or claim risk is high

protocol.objective: Check whether gathered evidence is enough to support PRD readiness and acceptance criteria.
protocol.prompt: Verify that no assumption is presented as fact, evidence paths/sources are usable, and unresolved missing data is reflected as blockers or assumptions.

evidence_tasks.reason: Verify blockers and assumptions are not promoted to facts.

state.next_action: Run explorer + cross-checker + verifier before treating project claims as facts.

agent_results.claim: YOLO provider dispatch API/state handling does not drift between Claude, Codex, and custom provider assumptions in demand evidence mode. The provider-adapter and evidence-dispatch implementations maintain provider-specific isolation while preserving full tool capability under harness boundary.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation function implements separate code paths for each provider type (codex, custom, claude) with provider-specific argument construction and no shared state leakage between providers.
evidence.why: This is the core dispatch logic that ensures provider-specific isolation; each provider has its own branch with dedicated config handling.

evidence.summary: Codex provider uses exec --cd --sandbox --approval-policy --output-last-message with codex_model override, completely separate from Claude argument patterns.
evidence.why: Demonstrates provider isolation: codex has its own execution model and output capture mechanism that doesn't leak into claude or custom providers.

evidence.summary: Custom provider uses sh -c with rendered command template, isolated from codex and claude argument patterns.
evidence.why: Shows custom provider has its own execution path that doesn't inherit assumptions from other providers.

evidence.summary: Claude provider uses -p --model --permission-mode --settings --tools --allowedTools --disallowedTools --disable-slash-commands --no-session-persistence --max-budget-usd, with no leakage from codex or custom provider patterns.
evidence.why: Demonstrates claude-specific argument construction that is isolated from other provider implementations.

evidence.summary: spawnProviderPrompt function is provider-agnostic: it delegates to buildProviderInvocation and handles stdout/stderr capture uniformly across providers without assuming provider-specific behavior.
evidence.why: This shows the API boundary: spawnProviderPrompt doesn't embed provider-specific assumptions; it delegates to provider-specific logic.

evidence.summary: executionConfig function normalizes provider-specific settings: explicit provider/model selection, custom_command handling, and agent_tool_profile isolation per provider type.
evidence.why: Demonstrates evidence dispatch maintains provider separation at configuration time, preventing cross-provider contamination.

evidence.summary: executionConfig explicitly prevents codex from inheriting claude default model: if provider is codex and model contains 'claude', the model field is cleared to avoid cross-provider model leakage.
evidence.why: This is explicit protection against cross-provider model leakage, directly addressing the demand concern.

evidence.summary: Claude provider configuration normalizes agent_tool_profile into three modes (research, boundary, full) and clears settings/tools flags to rely on boundary enforcement rather than tool restrictions.
evidence.why: Shows that claude evidence mode preserves full tool capability while using agent_tool_profile for semantic labeling, not tool restriction.

evidence.summary: Custom provider sets custom_sandbox to 'boundary' for demand evidence mode, isolating custom agent execution under the same boundary contract.
evidence.why: Demonstrates that custom provider is also subject to boundary enforcement in evidence mode.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot uses git ls-files and filesystem walking to create a SHA256 digest snapshot of all project files before agent execution, with allowed_write_roots exclusion.
evidence.why: This is the core boundary enforcement mechanism that applies equally to all providers.

evidence.summary: diffBoundarySnapshots compares before/after digests to detect any project mutations (added, modified, deleted files) regardless of which provider was used.
evidence.why: Shows boundary violation detection is provider-agnostic, catching mutations from any provider.

evidence.summary: runDemandEvidenceDispatchRuntime executes evidence agents with boundary snapshots before/after, then runs boundary diff and converts any changes into BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blockers in readiness.
evidence.why: Demonstrates the complete boundary enforcement workflow that applies to all providers equally.

evidence.summary: Boundary violations are added directly to readiness blockers, preventing prd_ready when any provider violates the boundary.
evidence.why: Shows that boundary violations from any provider become hard blockers that prevent PRD readiness.

evidence.summary: Test 'codex dispatch does not inherit a claude default model or force read-only sandbox' verifies codex provider isolation and that provider-specific config doesn't leak from other providers.
evidence.why: Executable test evidence confirming cross-provider leakage protection.

evidence.summary: Test 'claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on boundary enforcement' confirms claude evidence mode preserves full tool capability under boundary protection.
evidence.why: Test evidence that tool capability is preserved while boundary provides safety instead of tool restrictions.

evidence.summary: Test 'claude full tool profile requires explicit authorization for full semantic mode' verifies agent_tool_profile isolation and explicit authorization gating.
evidence.why: Test evidence showing tool profile modes are isolated and require explicit authorization.

evidence.summary: Test 'boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass' confirms boundary enforcement works regardless of provider or agent result quality.
evidence.why: Test evidence that boundary is provider-agnostic and catches mutations from any source.

evidence.summary: Test 'git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations' verifies boundary works with git repositories and catches mutations from any provider.
evidence.why: Test evidence showing boundary enforcement integrates with git and is provider-independent.

assumptions: The git status showing modified files represents the current working state of the demand evidence system
Claude Code CLI '-p' flag behavior matches the provider-adapter implementation assumptions
Codex CLI '--output-last-message' flag behavior is consistent across versions
risks: If Claude Code changes its '-p' flag behavior or tool permission model, the boundary enforcement might need updates
If Codex changes its output-last-message behavior, the evidence extraction logic might break
Custom provider commands that mutate project files outside the allowed_write_roots would be caught by boundary but might cause confusion

result.notes: Provider dispatch API/state handling is properly isolated between Claude, Codex, and custom providers. Each provider has its own execution path in buildProviderInvocation. executionConfig explicitly prevents cross-provider model leakage. Boundary enforcement via buildBoundarySnapshot/diffBoundarySnapshots applies equally to all providers and converts violations into readiness blockers. Test coverage confirms isolation, tool capability preservation, and boundary enforcement across providers.

agent_results.claim: Confirmed: YOLO provider dispatch maintains complete isolation between Claude, Codex, and custom providers. The buildProviderInvocation function implements separate code paths for each provider type with no shared state leakage. executionConfig explicitly prevents cross-provider model inheritance by clearing codex model field when it contains claude. Claude evidence mode preserves full tool capability using agent_tool_profile modes while boundary enforcement provides safety instead of tool restrictions. The buildBoundarySnapshot/diffBoundarySnapshots system applies provider-agnostic mutation detection that converts any project file changes into readiness blockers.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation implements three completely separate branches: lines 43-61 for codex (exec, --cd, --sandbox, --output-last-message), lines 63-75 for custom (sh -c), lines 77-108 for claude (-p with distinct flags). No provider inherits argument patterns from another.
evidence.why: This is the core isolation mechanism proving providers have independent code paths with no shared state.

evidence.summary: codexModel variable is computed separately from modelValue(ai): const codexModel = cleanString(ai.codex_model) || modelValue(ai), then used only for codex provider via --model flag splice.
evidence.why: Shows codex model handling is isolated from general model value handling.

evidence.summary: executionConfig contains explicit anti-leakage logic: if ((ai.provider || ai.executor) === 'codex') { if (!model && !clean(ai.codex_model) && /\bclaude\b/i.test(clean(ai.model))) ai.model = ''; }
evidence.why: This is the direct evidence that cross-provider model leakage is explicitly prevented, not just assumed.

evidence.summary: Claude provider configuration normalizes agent_tool_profile into research/boundary/full modes, then explicitly clears all tool restriction flags: ai.settings = ''; ai.claude_tools = ''; ai.claude_allowed_tools = ''; ai.claude_disallowed_tools = '';
evidence.why: Proves claude evidence mode preserves full tool capability by clearing restrictive flags rather than adding them.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot uses git ls-files and filesystem walking to create SHA256 digest map of all project files, excluding allowed_write_roots: if (allowed.some((root) => isWithin(absolute, root))) continue;
evidence.why: Shows boundary enforcement is file-system based and provider-agnostic.

evidence.summary: diffBoundarySnapshots iterates both before/after maps to detect added, modified, deleted files: for (const [file, digest] of before.entries()) { if (!after.has(file)) changes.push({ path: file, change: 'deleted' }); else if (after.get(file) !== digest) changes.push({ path: file, change: 'modified' }); }
evidence.why: Demonstrates provider-agnostic change detection that works regardless of which provider caused mutations.

evidence.summary: runDemandEvidenceDispatchRuntime calls buildBoundarySnapshot before and after all agents, then diffBoundarySnapshots to detect changes, then converts changes to BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blockers added to readiness.blockers array.
evidence.why: Shows complete workflow that applies boundary enforcement uniformly across all providers.

evidence.summary: Test 'codex dispatch does not inherit a claude default model or force read-only sandbox' asserts: assert.equal(/claude/i.test(runOptions.config.ai.model || ''), false);
evidence.why: Executable verification that cross-provider model leakage protection works as designed.

evidence.summary: Test 'claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on boundary enforcement' verifies claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, claude_disallowed_tools are all empty strings.
evidence.why: Test evidence confirming full tool capability preservation under boundary protection.

evidence.summary: Test 'boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass' shows writeFileSync(join(root, 'unexpected-project-write.txt'), 'mutation\n') causes result.status = 'blocked' and BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blocker.
evidence.why: Test proving boundary enforcement works regardless of agent result quality or provider type.

evidence.summary: Test 'git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations' uses git init + writeFileSync for untracked file, verifies boundary catches mutation and adds BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blocker.
evidence.why: Test evidence showing boundary integrates with git and catches mutations from any source.

assumptions: Git ls-files command behavior is consistent across git versions for boundary snapshot creation
SHA256 file digests reliably detect content changes for boundary enforcement
Provider CLI flags (-p for claude, exec for codex) maintain stable semantics across versions
risks: If provider CLIs change flag behavior (e.g., claude -p changes semantics or codex exec changes argument order), the provider-specific isolation could weaken
If git ls-files behavior changes, buildBoundarySnapshot might miss files or include incorrect files
If file system timing issues cause race conditions between snapshot creation and agent execution, boundary enforcement could miss mutations

result.notes: Independent verification confirms all explorer claims. Provider isolation is architecturally sound: buildProviderInvocation has separate branches for each provider with no shared state. executionConfig line 447-448 explicitly clears claude model from codex to prevent leakage. Claude evidence mode preserves full tool capability by clearing restriction flags rather than adding them. Boundary enforcement via buildBoundarySnapshot/diffBoundarySnapshots applies equally to all providers and converts violations into blockers. Test coverage validates isolation, cross-leakage prevention, tool capability preservation, and boundary enforcement. No conflicts found between explorer evidence and independent code analysis.

agent_results.claim: Provider dispatch isolation and boundary enforcement is factually verified with project-scoped evidence. All required PRD slots have evidence support. Assumptions are properly separated from facts and reflect reasonable external dependencies.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation has three separate code paths (codex: 43-61, custom: 63-75, claude: 77-108) proving provider isolation.
evidence.why: This is the architectural foundation for the claim that provider dispatch does not drift between providers.

evidence.summary: Explicit anti-leakage logic: if (provider is codex and model contains claude) ai.model = '' prevents cross-provider model inheritance.
evidence.why: Direct evidence that the demand concern (cross-provider leakage) is explicitly addressed in code.

evidence.summary: Claude evidence mode clears tool restriction flags (claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, claude_disallowed_tools) to preserve full tool capability.
evidence.why: Evidence that acceptance criteria (tool capability preservation under boundary) is implemented.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots implement provider-agnostic mutation detection using SHA256 digests.
evidence.why: Evidence that boundary enforcement applies equally to all providers regardless of implementation.

evidence.summary: Test suite covers provider isolation, cross-leakage prevention, tool capability preservation, and boundary enforcement.
evidence.why: Executable evidence that all claims are validated by automated tests.

evidence.summary: runDemandEvidenceDispatchRuntime integrates boundary snapshots before/after agent execution and converts violations into readiness blockers.
evidence.why: Evidence that boundary violations become hard blockers preventing PRD readiness.

assumptions: Git ls-files behavior is consistent across versions for boundary snapshot creation
Provider CLI flags (-p for claude, exec for codex) maintain stable semantics
SHA256 digests reliably detect content changes for mutation detection
risks: If provider CLIs change flag behavior, provider-specific isolation could weaken
If git ls-files behavior changes, boundary snapshot might miss files
Race conditions between snapshot creation and agent execution could miss mutations

result.notes: Explorer and cross-checker evidence agrees completely. All project claims are backed by verifiable code and test evidence with precise paths and line numbers. Assumptions are properly disclosed and reflect reasonable external dependencies on git and provider CLI behavior. No missing data. All acceptance criteria (provider isolation, cross-leakage prevention, tool capability preservation, boundary enforcement) have direct implementation evidence and test coverage. PRD readiness requirements are satisfied.
